title: Wild Pitch: How Far Do You Go?

root.narration: You're on second base with one out, trailing by a run. The count is 1-2 on the batter. The pitcher delivers a curveball that bounces in the dirt and skips past the catcher! Wild pitch! The ball rolls toward the backstop -- about 30 feet behind home plate. The catcher spins and chases it. You were already taking your secondary lead. How far do you advance?

choices.text: Sprint to third! A wild pitch from second base should ALWAYS mean advancing to third. Read how far the ball went and take the base!
Go for home! The ball went all the way to the backstop -- I can score the tying run right now!
Stay at second. I don't want to run into an out. The ball might not have gone that far.

decision-at-third.narration: You sprint to third base easily -- the catcher is still chasing the ball at the backstop. You pull up at third with the base standing up. Now you're 90 feet from home with one out. The catcher retrieves the ball and throws it back to the pitcher. But you notice something: the backstop at this field is SHORT -- only about 20 feet behind the plate. If another wild pitch happens, the catcher can get to it quickly. The batter is still hitting with a 2-2 count now. What's your plan from third?

choices.text: Take an aggressive secondary lead in foul territory. If the pitcher throws another one in the dirt, I'll score easily. If the batter hits it, I'm scoring on anything.
Take a short lead and stay close to the bag. I don't want to get picked off after just getting here.
Watch the pitcher's release point. If I see a curveball spinning, I'll break for home immediately because curveballs are more likely to be wild.

outcome-aggressive-lead-great.narration: You take a walking lead in foul territory as the pitcher delivers. The batter swings and hits a grounder to the second baseman. You're already moving toward home with your aggressive secondary lead! The second baseman fields it and throws to first for the out -- he can't throw home because you're already three-quarters of the way there. You score STANDING UP! TIE GAME! Your aggressive lead from third turned a routine grounder into a run!

outcome.headline: Run Scores! Aggressive Lead From Third!
outcome.explanation: This is why the secondary lead from third base is so important. With an aggressive walking lead in foul territory (so you don't get hit by a batted ball), you're already in motion when the ball is put in play. On a grounder to the right side (first baseman or second baseman), the fielder's natural throw goes to first base -- AWAY from home. With your momentum already going, the fielder can't pivot and throw home in time. The keys to a great third-base lead: 1) Start in foul territory to avoid interference. 2) Use a walking lead -- take secondary steps as the pitcher delivers. 3) On grounders to the right side, you're scoring. 4) On grounders to the left side, read and react. Your lead from third base is directly connected to how often you score.
outcome.whatToRemember: From third base, take an aggressive walking lead in foul territory. The more momentum you have as the pitch arrives, the more likely you score on grounders, wild pitches, and passed balls.

outcome-conservative-okay.narration: You stay close to the bag with a small lead. The batter grounds out to the second baseman -- routine play to first. You don't score because you were flat-footed at third with no momentum. The fielder didn't even look at you. Two outs now, you're on third, and you need a HIT to score instead of just a grounder to the right side. Your conservative lead cost you a chance to tie the game.

outcome.headline: Didn't Score. Conservative Lead Hurt.
outcome.explanation: From third base, your lead is everything. A short, flat-footed lead means you can only score on clean hits to the outfield. An aggressive walking lead means you can score on grounders to the right side, wild pitches, passed balls, and sac flies. The difference is enormous. Getting picked off at third is rare because the pitcher has to disengage the rubber and make a difficult throw. The reward of an aggressive lead (scoring on more plays) far outweighs the risk of getting picked off (which almost never happens). Don't waste the scoring position by being timid.
outcome.whatToRemember: Don't be conservative at third base. The risk of getting picked off is tiny compared to the reward of scoring on ground balls and wild pitches. Take an aggressive lead and be in motion when the pitch is delivered.

outcome-break-dirt-good.narration: You watch for the curveball spin. The pitcher throws -- it looks like a curveball! You break for home immediately! But wait -- it's a changeup, not a curveball, and it catches the inside corner for a STRIKE. The batter doesn't swing. The catcher catches it cleanly and you're caught in a rundown between home and third! After two throws, they tag you out. You read the pitch wrong and broke too early.

outcome.headline: Caught in Rundown! Wrong Read on the Pitch!
outcome.explanation: The idea of reading the pitch type from the runner's perspective is advanced thinking, but it's extremely difficult to execute. From 90 feet away at third base, distinguishing a curveball from a changeup is nearly impossible -- they can look similar out of the hand. Breaking for home based on the pitch type is risky because: 1) You might misread the pitch. 2) Even if it IS a curveball, it might not be wild. 3) You're committing before you know the outcome. A better approach: take your aggressive secondary lead and react to what happens AFTER the pitch arrives. If it's wild, you score. If it's not, you hold. Let the RESULT tell you what to do, not your prediction.
outcome.whatToRemember: Don't break for home based on predicting the pitch type. React to the RESULT -- if the ball gets past the catcher, go. If it doesn't, hold. Predictions are risky; reactions are reliable.

outcome-try-home-bad.narration: You sprint all the way from second trying to score on the wild pitch! The ball only went about 30 feet behind the plate. The catcher grabs it quickly, spins, and you're still 30 feet from home. He's waiting for you with the ball. You try to stop but your momentum carries you toward home. The catcher tags you easily. OUT! You tried to go from second to home on a wild pitch that didn't go far enough. That's 180 feet of running on a ball that only went 30 feet past the catcher.

outcome.headline: OUT at Home! Too Aggressive!
outcome.explanation: Going from second to home on a wild pitch requires the ball to go FAR past the catcher -- at least 40-50 feet -- or the backstop to be very far from the plate. You have to cover 180 feet (second to home), while the catcher only has to cover 30 feet (to the ball and back). The math doesn't work unless the ball goes deep. The smart play from second on a wild pitch is to advance ONE base to third. From third, you're in scoring position and can score on the NEXT wild pitch, a sac fly, a grounder, or a hit. Going two bases on one wild pitch almost never works because the distance is too great.
outcome.whatToRemember: On a wild pitch from second base, advance ONE base to third. Going all the way home requires the ball to travel very far past the catcher. Take one base at a time -- the scoring position is valuable enough.

outcome-stay-bad.narration: You stay at second base. The ball went 30 feet past the catcher and you didn't move. The catcher retrieves it casually, jogs back to the plate, and the at-bat continues. You wasted a FREE base. A wild pitch with a runner on second should result in the runner advancing to third -- it's almost automatic. By staying, you left yourself further from scoring and showed the defense you're not a threat on the bases.

outcome.headline: Stayed at Second! Free Base Wasted!
outcome.explanation: A wild pitch is a FREE base -- the defense made a mistake, and you should make them pay for it. Staying at second on a wild pitch that bounces 30 feet behind the plate is one of the worst baserunning decisions you can make. The key is your SECONDARY LEAD. When you take a proper secondary lead (shuffling toward third as the pitcher delivers), you're already in motion when the ball gets past the catcher. If you're standing flat-footed on the base, you see the wild pitch, THEN start running, and you're already a step behind. The secondary lead turns reaction time into momentum.
outcome.whatToRemember: ALWAYS advance on a wild pitch from second base. Take your secondary lead so you're already moving when the ball gets past the catcher. A wild pitch is a gift -- take the free base.
